title: Why Split Data Three Ways?
concept: Splitting data into training, validation, and test sets prevents hyperparameter tuning from leaking test information into the model.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which technique helps balance learning from imbalanced data by scaling up cost function penalties for rare class errors?
exercises.explain: Correct! Class weighting scales up the penalty for minority class errors, preventing the model from simply ignoring rare cases. Now, let's learn how to split our data to evaluate models properly!

cards.title: Studying for the exam
cards.body: Imagine you're studying for a big exam. If you study with the exact questions that will be on the test, you'll score 100%. But does that prove you actually learned the subject, or did you just memorize the answers?

cards.title: The three-way split
cards.body: To evaluate honestly, we split data into three parts: Training (to teach the model), Validation (to tune settings like learning rate), and Test (to run a final, unbiased exam).

cards.title: The practice test
cards.body: Think of the training set as your textbook, the validation set as a practice quiz to see if you need to study differently, and the test set as the final exam you only take once.

cards.title: Data leakage in action
cards.body: If you use the test set to choose your best model settings (hyperparameters), information from the test set leaks into your decision. The model will look great on paper but fail in the real world.

cards.title: Why validation is not test
cards.body: Many think validation and test sets are the same. But because we actively use the validation set to tweak and select the best model, it is no longer an unbiased judge. Only the test set remains untouched.

cards.title: Validation vs Test Sets
cards.body: Understanding the roles of our tuning and testing data:

left.text:
Used during training.
Helps tune hyperparameters.
Influences model choice.

right.text:
Kept locked away.
Used once at the very end.
Provides unbiased final score.

exercises.prompt: Why must we keep the test set completely separate from the model until the very end?

options.text: To ensure our final evaluation is unbiased and reflects real-world performance
To speed up the training process by using less data
To allow the model to learn from the test set during validation
Because the test set contains different features than the training set

exercises.explain: If the model or the developer sees the test set during tuning, test information leaks into the design, leading to overly optimistic results.

exercises.prompt: True or false: It is acceptable to use the test set to adjust hyperparameters as long as the model doesn't train on it directly.
exercises.explain: False! Adjusting hyperparameters based on the test set is a form of data leakage. You are choosing settings that work well for that specific test set.

exercises.sentence: We use the ___ set to train the model, the validation set to tune hyperparameters, and the test set for the final evaluation.
exercises.explain: The training set is the primary data the model learns from, while the other sets serve evaluation purposes.

exercises.prompt: Which of these workflow steps describes a clean evaluation process with NO data leakage?

options.text: Splitting data into train/val/test, training on train, choosing the best settings using val, and checking the final score on test.
Trying 10 different models on the test set and selecting the one that scores the highest.
Training on the training set, adjusting learning rates to maximize test accuracy, and reporting that test score.

exercises.explain: In a clean workflow, the test set is never used to make decisions. It is only read once to measure final performance.

exercises.prompt: You train a linear regression model to predict stock prices. You split your data into Train, Validation, and Test. Your model gets 95% accuracy on Train and 92% on Validation. After tuning your settings on Validation, you run it on the Test set and get 60%. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: You overfitted to the validation set during tuning, or information leaked from the training process.
The test set was trained on for too many epochs.
The validation set was too small to be used for training.
You forgot to normalize the test set weights.

exercises.explain: Over-tuning settings to maximize validation performance can cause validation-overfitting. This is why we need that untouched final Test set to catch these issues before deploying! 🚀
